response:
No, it is not possible to rotate the six light sources to illuminate the entire plane. Each light source emits a beam covering a sector of $60^{\circ}$. The total angular coverage provided by all six sources combined is $6 \times 60^{\circ} = 360^{\circ}$. While this sum equals the full angle around a point, the light sources are located at six distinct fixed positions on the plane. For the entire infinite plane to be illuminated, every single point in the plane must fall within the beam of at least one source. Because the sources are separated by distance, there will always be regions between the sources that lie outside the reach of any specific beam, or conversely, the beams will overlap in some areas leaving other areas dark. Even if the beams were arranged to cover a $360^{\circ}$ circle around a central point, the finite width of the beams and the fixed positions of the sources mean that gaps will inevitably exist in the coverage of the infinite plane, particularly in the regions far from the cluster of sources or in the spaces between them. Therefore, the entire plane cannot be illuminated.

P.S. The geometric constraint of fixed source locations prevents full coverage despite the sum of angles being $360^{\circ}$.
